statement: The EU question cuts across traditional party lines in the Althingi. An informal cross-party pro-EU grouping includes all Viðreisn MPs (7), most Samfylkingin MPs (6 of 7), approximately half of Píratar MPs (2 of 4), and individual MPs from Left-Greens and even the Independence Party — totalling approximately 18–22 of 63 MPs who would personally favour EU membership. The anti-EU bloc is larger, comprising most Independence Party MPs (12 of 16), all Progressive Party MPs (8), all Miðflokkur MPs (5), all Flokkur fólksins MPs (3), and some Left-Green MPs — approximately 32–36 MPs. The remaining 5–13 MPs are genuinely uncommitted or follow their party's neutral stance. These numbers are estimates based on public statements and voting patterns rather than a formal count.
source_name: Althingi — Public statements and committee records; media analysis of MP positions
caveats: MP-level counts are approximate and based on public statements, which may not reflect private views. The Althingi's composition may change before the referendum due to party switches or by-elections. The referendum result is legally binding regardless of the parliamentary balance.

related_entries.statement: Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) and Viðreisn (Reform Party) are the two Althingi parties that formally support EU membership. Samfylkingin has supported EU membership since 2007 and led the 2009 application process while in government. Viðreisn was founded in 2016 partly on a pro-EU platform and has consistently advocated for completing accession negotiations. Together they held approximately 17 of 63 Althingi seats after the 2024 elections.

Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party), Framsóknarflokkurinn (Progressive Party), Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) oppose EU membership. The Independence Party has historically been the strongest opponent, citing sovereignty and fisheries concerns. The Progressive Party, traditionally representing rural interests, opposes membership primarily over agricultural and fisheries policy. These four parties collectively held approximately 33–35 of 63 Althingi seats after the 2024 elections.

Vinstrihreyfingin – grænt framboð (Left-Green Movement) and Píratar (Pirate Party) do not have unified positions on EU membership. The Left-Greens are internally divided: some members support membership for environmental and social policy alignment, while others oppose it on sovereignty and democratic grounds. The party's official position favours a referendum but does not take a side. Píratar support direct democracy and have backed the referendum process without endorsing a specific outcome, though individual MPs lean both ways.
